title: VPN on Arch Linux: Split Tunneling, Kill Switch with iptables, and WireGuard Config
description: Advanced VPN configuration on Arch Linux. Covers WireGuard setup via the AUR and official repos, writing a kill switch with iptables PostUp/PreDown directives, per-application split tunneling with network namespaces, and DNS leak prevention.
content:
## What Makes Arch Linux Different

Arch Linux gives you more control over network configuration than any mainstream distribution, and that cuts both ways. WireGuard is in the official `[extra]` repository. NetworkManager, systemd-networkd, and raw `wg-quick` all work. There is no VPN daemon forcing its routing rules on you.

This guide assumes you are past basic WireGuard setup and want to understand the mechanics: how a kill switch works at the iptables level, how split tunneling via network namespaces works, and how to prevent DNS leaks on Arch's systemd-resolved setup.

## Installing WireGuard

```bash
sudo pacman -S wireguard-tools
```

That is the only package needed. The `wireguard` kernel module ships with the Linux kernel (5.6+). Arch's rolling release means you always have a current kernel, so the module is always there. Verify: `lsmod | grep wireguard`, or check with `modinfo wireguard`.

For VPN provider apps:

- **NordVPN**: `yay -S nordvpn-bin` (AUR, official binary package from NordVPN's repo)
- **ProtonVPN**: `yay -S proton-vpn-gtk-app` (AUR, GUI) or `yay -S protonvpn-cli-ng` for CLI
- **Mullvad**: `yay -S mullvad-vpn-bin` (official Mullvad binary from AUR)

For manual WireGuard configs without a provider app, no AUR package is needed.

## WireGuard Config and wg-quick

Place your config at `/etc/wireguard/wg0.conf`. A minimal config:

```ini
[Interface]
PrivateKey = <your_private_key>
Address = 10.5.0.2/32
DNS = 103.86.96.100

[Peer]
PublicKey = <server_public_key>
Endpoint = 194.165.16.1:51820
AllowedIPs = 0.0.0.0/0, ::/0
PersistentKeepalive = 25
```

`AllowedIPs = 0.0.0.0/0, ::/0` routes all IPv4 and IPv6 traffic through the tunnel. This is full-tunnel mode.

For split tunneling (only specific traffic through the VPN), replace with the IP ranges you want tunneled:

```ini
AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.0/8, 192.168.1.0/24
```

This sends RFC 1918 ranges through the tunnel and leaves all other traffic on the default route.

## Kill Switch via iptables in wg-quick

The kill switch problem: if the WireGuard tunnel drops, traffic fails over to the regular interface and your real IP is exposed. A proper kill switch blocks all traffic that is not going through the tunnel.

Add `PostUp` and `PreDown` directives to your wg-quick config:

```ini
[Interface]
PrivateKey = <your_private_key>
Address = 10.5.0.2/32
DNS = 103.86.96.100
PostUp = iptables -I OUTPUT ! -o %i -m mark ! --mark $(wg show %i fwmark) -m addrtype ! --dst-type LOCAL -j REJECT
PostUp = iptables -I OUTPUT ! -o %i -m mark ! --mark $(wg show %i fwmark) -m addrtype ! --dst-type LOCAL -j REJECT -p tcp
PreDown = iptables -D OUTPUT ! -o %i -m mark ! --mark $(wg show %i fwmark) -m addrtype ! --dst-type LOCAL -j REJECT
PreDown = iptables -D OUTPUT ! -o %i -m mark ! --mark $(wg show %i fwmark) -m addrtype ! --dst-type LOCAL -j REJECT -p tcp

[Peer]
PublicKey = <server_public_key>
Endpoint = 194.165.16.1:51820
AllowedIPs = 0.0.0.0/0
PersistentKeepalive = 25
```

`%i` is substituted with the interface name (`wg0`) by wg-quick. `$(wg show %i fwmark)` gets WireGuard's internal firewall mark, which identifies packets that are already inside the tunnel. The rule rejects any outgoing packet that is not going through `wg0` and is not marked by WireGuard itself.

For IPv6 kill switch, duplicate the rules using `ip6tables`:

```ini
PostUp = ip6tables -I OUTPUT ! -o %i -m mark ! --mark $(wg show %i fwmark) -m addrtype ! --dst-type LOCAL -j REJECT
PreDown = ip6tables -D OUTPUT ! -o %i -m mark ! --mark $(wg show %i fwmark) -m addrtype ! --dst-type LOCAL -j REJECT
```

Test the kill switch: bring up the tunnel with `sudo wg-quick up wg0`, then manually take down the WireGuard interface `sudo ip link set wg0 down`. Try to curl a URL. It should time out or be rejected. Restore with `sudo ip link set wg0 up`.

## DNS Leak Prevention on Arch with systemd-resolved

Arch Linux commonly uses systemd-resolved. When wg-quick sets the DNS from the config file's `DNS =` line, it calls `resolvconf` or directly modifies `/etc/resolv.conf`. If systemd-resolved is running in stub mode, it intercepts DNS on `127.0.0.53`. DNS queries from other interfaces can leak past the VPN's DNS server.

Fix this by configuring systemd-resolved to handle the VPN DNS properly:

```bash
# Check current resolved status
resolvectl status

# After wg-quick up wg0, check if the VPN DNS is active on wg0
resolvectl dns wg0
```

To force all DNS through the VPN interface's DNS server, add to `/etc/systemd/resolved.conf`:

```ini
[Resolve]
DNS=103.86.96.100
DNSOverTLS=opportunistic
DNSSEC=allow-downgrade
```

Alternatively, configure the wg-quick config to explicitly set per-interface DNS via resolvconf. Install `openresolv`: `sudo pacman -S openresolv`. Then in wg-quick, the `DNS =` directive works through resolvconf, which handles systemd-resolved correctly.

## Split Tunneling with Network Namespaces

Network namespace-based split tunneling lets you run specific applications through the VPN while the rest of the system uses the regular connection. This is more reliable than policy routing because it is per-process, not per-IP.

### Create the VPN namespace

```bash
# Create a namespace named 'vpn'
sudo ip netns add vpn

# Create a virtual Ethernet pair to bridge host and namespace
sudo ip link add veth0 type veth peer name veth1

# Assign veth1 to the vpn namespace
sudo ip link set veth1 netns vpn

# Configure IPs
sudo ip addr add 10.200.0.1/24 dev veth0
sudo ip link set veth0 up
sudo ip netns exec vpn ip addr add 10.200.0.2/24 dev veth1
sudo ip netns exec vpn ip link set veth1 up
sudo ip netns exec vpn ip link set lo up

# Add default route inside namespace through host
sudo ip netns exec vpn ip route add default via 10.200.0.1

# Enable NAT for namespace traffic on the host
sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s 10.200.0.0/24 -j MASQUERADE
sudo sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
```

### Move WireGuard into the namespace

```bash
# Create a WireGuard interface in the namespace
sudo ip netns exec vpn ip link add wg0 type wireguard
sudo ip netns exec vpn ip link set wg0 up

# Apply config
sudo ip netns exec vpn wg setconf wg0 /etc/wireguard/wg0.conf
sudo ip netns exec vpn ip addr add 10.5.0.2/32 dev wg0
sudo ip netns exec vpn ip route add default dev wg0
```

### Run an application inside the namespace

```bash
# Run Firefox through the VPN
sudo ip netns exec vpn sudo -u $USER firefox

# Run a terminal through the VPN
sudo ip netns exec vpn sudo -u $USER /bin/bash

# Check IP inside the namespace
sudo ip netns exec vpn curl https://ipinfo.io/ip
```

Your regular desktop session uses the normal internet connection. Only processes explicitly launched in the `vpn` namespace go through the tunnel. This is how to run one browser with a VPN and another without, simultaneously.

## Persisting WireGuard with systemd

```bash
# Enable auto-start
sudo systemctl enable wg-quick@wg0
sudo systemctl start wg-quick@wg0

# Check status
sudo systemctl status wg-quick@wg0

# View logs
journalctl -u wg-quick@wg0 -f
```

## Using NetworkManager Instead of wg-quick

If you prefer NetworkManager (common on Arch desktop installs):

```bash
sudo pacman -S networkmanager-openvpn
# For WireGuard, NM has native support since NM 1.16
nmcli connection import type wireguard file /etc/wireguard/wg0.conf
nmcli connection up wg0
```

NetworkManager integration means the VPN connection shows up in the system tray and can be controlled with `nmcli` or a GUI.

## Recommendation

For Arch Linux users who want a commercial VPN that supports advanced configuration, [NordVPN](https://nordvpn.com) is a good fit. The `nordvpn-bin` AUR package installs cleanly, the NordLynx (WireGuard) implementation is fast, and the CLI exposes kill switch and split tunneling controls without needing to write iptables rules manually. If you prefer to manage everything yourself with raw WireGuard configs, Mullvad and ProtonVPN both export standard `.conf` files that work with the patterns above without modification.
